paragraphs.text: The unfair commercial practices leave a lot to be desired as well as answered for by all of the companies in question. While many may not share this view, I have the perspective that video games are an art form. I've found that certain video games can have the same emotional effect on me as say a revered Renaissance painting, an early modern work of literature, or a film from the golden age of cinema, among other comparisons. Either way, they are works of human expression and labor that I would argue are highly valuable to future generations in the same way that other art forms can be. Now it's true not every video game fills this description, but that doesn't mean those games deserve to be relegated to the metaphorical bin of oblivion. But for both games that do and games that don't, they shouldn't be locked behind digital bars that have to be maintained by groups with a financial incentive to keep them going. Once the financial incentive is gone then why should they have any reason to preserve the medium? They have none. And so both the creative labor and the psychological labor and the all of the time and soul and spirit sunk into the creation of a modern video game go to waste. Would you say this is fair, and reasonable? No, of course not. While it is understandable that those who fund, produce, and facilitate the creation of these games would want to protect their financial interests within a certain time frame, the fact that they aren't beholden to any authority once their financial interest is gone to keep the work alive is a heinous crime to the human creative spirit. Something needs to be done and enshrined in law in order to fix this gap in human justice.
